svg.title: PowerMOD full power state machine
svg.desc:
Detailed state diagram of PowerMOD's power control, with five states: OFF, POWERING_ON, ON, SHUTDOWN_PENDING, and EMERGENCY_CUTOFF.
Powering the host on: from OFF, any of these enters POWERING_ON, which advances to ON once the output rail is stable. Triggers are an RTC alarm coming due for an armed wake; a missed alarm that came due while the board was unpowered, firing as soon as power returns; a button press; power being restored, if the power-on mode is Always-ON or is Restore-previous-state and the host was ON when power was lost; the opt-in max-sleep safety timeout; and watchdog recovery — the automatic re-power that follows a watchdog-forced cut, once the minimum off-duration has elapsed. An RTC alarm arriving while the host is already ON is a no-op, and because the host counts as ON until power is actually cut, this also covers the shutdown-delay race.
Powering the host off: four triggers enter SHUTDOWN_PENDING identically, where a delay-before-cut timer runs. They are: the host writing a power-off request; a button press; battery voltage falling below the cutoff floor; and an opt-in watchdog timeout expiring. The trigger changes only two things — which state is entered when the delay elapses, and what can cancel it: a second button press cancels a button-initiated shutdown, a late watchdog kick cancels a watchdog-initiated one, and nothing cancels a host-requested cut or a low-battery cutoff. The two delays are separately configurable, and the host-requested one defaults to zero.
There is a fifth path that skips SHUTDOWN_PENDING entirely: holding the button for five seconds cuts power immediately, with no delay and no cancellation. It is the deliberate "pull the plug" escape.
After any cut, power stays off for at least a minimum off-duration before any trigger may re-power the host. This is a separate, post-cut guard, distinct from the two pre-cut delays.
EMERGENCY_CUTOFF is entered from SHUTDOWN_PENDING when the recorded reason was low voltage, and is reachable only in LiPo mode — mains-only mode has no battery to monitor or protect. It returns to OFF once battery voltage rises above the recovery threshold or mains power is present — and because an emergency cutoff records the host as having been ON, the power-on mode then decides re-powering: Restore-previous-state (the default) and Always-ON boot the host; Always-OFF waits for a button or schedule. It requires no host cooperation and cannot be cancelled: the delay is a fixed grace window before an unconditional action, not a negotiation.
Wake times are evaluated when committed, not when they come due. A time already in the past at commit is marked expired and never fires; a future one is armed. A wake or max-sleep timeout coming due while the battery is below the recovery threshold with no mains present is held, and fires once power is usable; a button press below the cutoff floor does not boot the host at all. OFF with no armed wake stays OFF indefinitely unless the opt-in max-sleep timeout is set.

g.text: LiPo mode only — unreachable in mains-only mode,
where there is no battery to monitor or protect

svg.text: Missed alarm: wake came due while power
was out or unusable — fires when usable
Gate: alarms & max-sleep are held while
is actually cut, so this covers
entirely — ON straight to OFF, no delay,
Delay elapses, power cut — then OFF for at least MIN_OFF_DURATION
Voltage > recovery threshold, or mains present
SHUTDOWN_PENDING is identical regardless of trigger:
all four triggers start the same delay-before-cut timer. Only two things depend on which
one fired: the state entered when the delay elapses, and what can cancel it: a second
press (button-initiated) or a late kick (watchdog-initiated). Host-requested delay = 0.
A 5s button hold skips the state entirely and cuts immediately. After ANY cut, power stays
off for MIN_OFF_DURATION before any trigger may re-power — a separate, post-cut guard.
EMERGENCY_CUTOFF no longer bypasses the delay (revised):
the low-voltage cutoff routes through SHUTDOWN_PENDING like every other power-off —
the floor sits well above the board's own minimum operating voltage, so a hard cut buys
nothing. It still needs no host cooperation and still cannot be cancelled: the delay is a
fixed grace window before an unconditional action, not a negotiation.
On recovery the host counts as ON (it was cut involuntarily) — the default mode re-boots it.
A wake time is evaluated when committed, not when it comes due:
past-at-commit is marked expired and never fires; future-at-commit is armed, and fires on
power becoming usable — incl. battery back above recovery. OFF, no armed wake: OFF forever
unless the opt-in max-sleep timeout is set. Restore-previous-state is the default power-on
mode; under Always-OFF, power restore alone never triggers POWERING_ON.
